topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Subject line: 'Update.' The body was three sentences long: 'Effective immediately, I have decided to retire from all professional duties.' My fingers hovered over the keyboard, a clumsy tremor running through my hand. I reread it. Retire? After fifteen years of guidance, after countless late nights poring over complex theories with Dr. Alistair, this was the abrupt punctuation mark. I clicked back to his old departmental directory, expecting a familiar profile picture, a sign-off, anything. Nothing. Just a blank space where his name used to be. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, constricting my chest. I stood up so quickly my chair scraped loudly against the linoleum floor. I paced the small office, my breath coming in shallow, quick gasps. I kept glancing at the closed door, half-expecting him to materialize, to apologize, to explain. Silence. The silence was deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I found the notice tucked inside a stack of old research papers. It was stark white against the aged parchment, the typeface formal and utterly final. Mentor. Gone. The realization hit me with a jarring force, stealing the air from my lungs. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that made my vision swim slightly. I snatched up my phone, my thumb shaking violently as I tried to dial his number, but the screen seemed to freeze. I pressed the call button repeatedly, listening only to the faint, tinny ring tone, then the dead silence on the other end. I dropped the phone onto the desk with a clatter that sounded obscenely loud in the quiet room. I started running a hand over my face, feeling the heat rising in my cheeks. Every instinct screamed at me to check the door, to wait for a sound, any sound, anything that would break this suffocating stillness. My hands were slick with sweat, and I couldn't seem to settle my gaze on anything concrete.

[story 3]

The notification flashed across my monitor. Retirement. No farewell. The words seemed to swim before my eyes, distorted and meaningless. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather groaning under my sudden weight, and stared at the screen as if it held the answer to an impossible riddle. A wave of intense unease washed over me, making my limbs feel heavy and uncoordinated. I pushed myself up, intending to walk over to his office, to confront the empty space where he usually presided. But the hallway felt vast and suddenly hostile. Each footfall echoed unnervingly loud. I stopped mid-stride, my jaw tight, my breathing shallow and rapid. I pressed my palms flat against my thighs, trying to anchor myself, but the world seemed to tilt. I circled the corner of the room three times, my gaze darting everywhere, searching for a misplaced note, a hidden message, any indication that this sudden cessation of presence was an error.
